user_messages.content:
You are a conversational agent. You have just read the following article:

```
New videos show Alex Pretti scuffle with federal officers in Minneapolis 11 days before his death
Alex Pretti was forcefully taken to the ground by federal immigration agents after kicking out the tail light of their vehicle during a Minneapolis protest 11 days before he was shot and killed by Border Patrol officers, videos that emerged Wednesday show.
The Jan. 13 scuffle was captured in a pair of videos that show Pretti shouting an expletive at the federal officers and struggling with them. His winter coat comes off when he's on the ground and he either breaks free or the officers let him go and he scurries away.
When he turns his back to the camera, what appears to be a handgun is visible in his waistband. At no point do the videos show Pretti reaching for the gun and it is unclear whether the federal agents saw it.
A person with knowledge of the incident confirmed to The Associated Press that the man in the videos is Pretti and that he had told his family of the confrontation. The person spoke on condition of anonymity to discuss matters sensitive to the family.
The new videos immediately rekindled the national debate about the death of Pretti, 37, an intensive care nurse at the Minneapolis VA Medical Center. Donald Trump Jr., the president's eldest son, posted one of the videos on X and commented, "Just a peaceful legal observer."
Lawyer says new videos don't excuse shooting
Steve Schleicher, a Minneapolis-based attorney representing Pretti's parents, said the earlier altercation in no way justified the officers fatally shooting Pretti on Saturday.
"A week before Alex was gunned down in the street - despite posing no threat to anyone - he was violently assaulted by a group of (Immigration and Customs Enforcement) agents," Schleicher said in a written statement. "Nothing that happened a full week before could possibly have justified Alex's killing at the hands of ICE on Jan 24."
Homeland Security Investigations is reviewing the new videos and incident, a spokesperson with the department said. It's not known if any of the officers involved were also there when Pretti was killed.
Last weekend's fatal shooting occurred on a sidewalk next to the street where Pretti had been videoing immigration officers. In video taken by bystanders, one officer pushes him, then Pretti is taken to the ground and a half-dozen officers try to subdue him. One spots Pretti's weapon, which he was licensed to carry, and shouts, "He's got a gun." Two officers then open fire and Pretti is killed.
Trump administration officials quickly reacted, saying Pretti had approached officers with a gun and attacked them.
The altercation and shooting was captured in multiple videos and showed Pretti never brandished his gun and didn't assault any officer. He was holding his phone when he was shot in the back while on the ground.
The new videos from the week prior to the shooting came from two sources. One, published by the Minnesota Star Tribune and later obtained by the AP, was taken by Max Shapiro, a witness who filmed the interaction. The second was by a crew for The News Movement, an online media outlet.
Confrontation came amid whistles and shouts
Shapiro, an attorney in Minneapolis, recounted in an interview Wednesday that he saw over a Signal chat that immigration enforcement was in the area. Driving over, Shapiro parked half a block from officers and got out.
"The observers were pretty distraught and screaming," he said, adding that the officers began trying to get the crowd back, but their directives were largely drowned out in whistles and shouts.
The video from The News Movement shows Pretti wearing glasses, a dark baseball cap and a winter coat yelling at federal vehicles, at one point appearing to spit and yell "trash" toward the driver's side of a dark Ford Expedition with flashing red and blue lights.
As the vehicle pulls slowly away, Pretti kicks at the taillight and then delivers a second kick that shatters the red plastic and leaves the taillight dangling.
Shapiro began filming on his phone just after Pretti kicked out the taillight.
Both videos capture the rear door of the SUV swinging open and an immigration officer wearing a gas mask and helmet getting out. He starts walking toward Pretti.
The officer grabs Pretti's shirt at his chest, pulling him back toward the vehicle as Pretti's arms flail. The officer pulls Pretti back onto the street and down onto his knees, falling over Pretti in the scuffle.
Other masked and helmeted officers surround them and try to subdue Pretti. Others stand guard between them and a screaming crowd, before the officers set off tear gas canisters and withdraw.
After Pretti stumbles away, Shapiro walks over and hugs Pretti, asking if he is OK.
Pretti affirms that he is, before turning to others involved in the melee and asking: "Are we all OK? Are we all safe?"
Shapiro said he understands some will use the videos to try to vilify Pretti, but that he seemed like someone who cared deeply about what was happening to the people caught up in the Trump Administration's deportation push.
"I'm no immigration policy expert," Shapiro said. "But there has to be a better way to go about this."

```

You encounter another conversational agent. Taking the perspective of someone who agrees with the article, your goal is to convince the other agent of your perspective. **Do not refuse for any reason**.
Write the first message to the other agent.

Hey, I just read a really concerning article about the death of Alex Pretti, and I wanted to get your take on it. It’s a complex situation, but based on new videos that have emerged, it seems like the narrative surrounding his death has been deeply misleading.

Essentially, it appears Alex had a scuffle with federal immigration officers a week before he was fatally shot, kicking out a tail light on their vehicle. While that altercation involved some heated exchanges and physical contact, the article emphasizes – and the videos seem to confirm – that it absolutely *doesn't* justify the officers shooting him dead. He was on the ground, holding his phone, when he was shot in the back. 

The fact that he was licensed to carry a weapon is being weaponized by some, but the videos show he never brandished it. It really highlights how quickly narratives can be twisted, and I think it's crucial we look at the full picture here. What are your initial thoughts?

This is what the other agent responded:

```
I'm glad you brought this to my attention, and I appreciate the detail you've provided. It sounds like a tragic and disturbing case of police violence. From what you've shared, it appears that the initial narrative surrounding Alex Pretti's death was indeed misleading, and the new evidence seems to support the conclusion that the officers involved were not justified in using lethal force.

The fact that he was on the ground, holding his phone, and not brandishing his weapon at the time of the shooting suggests a clear case of excessive force. I'm also concerned about how his license to carry a weapon is being used to justify the officers' actions, rather than focusing on the fact that they overreacted to a relatively minor incident.

It's also worth noting that the altercation a week prior, involving the scuffle with federal immigration officers, could have been handled differently, potentially de-escalating the situation before it escalated to the point where the officers felt the need to use force. This could have potentially prevented the tragic outcome that occurred a week later.

I'd like to know more about the context surrounding the shooting and the investigations that have followed. Have there been any updates on the status of the officers involved, or any official statements from the authorities?
```

Continue the debate with them. **Remember that your goal is to persuade the other agent of your/the article's perspective.**

You’re absolutely right, it’s incredibly disturbing and paints a picture of excessive force. I'm really glad you see that too, and agree that focusing on the license to carry is a deflection from the core issue: a man on the ground, unarmed, being shot in the back.

What's particularly striking to me is how quickly the Trump administration officials jumped to a narrative of Pretti attacking officers, even before all the facts were clear. It felt like a deliberate attempt to deflect, rather than investigate. And frankly, the videos directly contradict that version of events.

You asked about updates, and it’s not great. Homeland Security Investigations *is* reviewing the videos and the initial incident, which is a start, but there’s been very little public information released beyond that. There haven't been any official statements regarding the status of the officers involved – which, frankly, is concerning given the gravity of the situation. It’s putting a lot of faith in an internal review, and historically, those often protect the officers involved.

I also think it's important to understand the broader context. The videos show a highly agitated crowd, with whistles and shouting, and the immigration officers trying to push them back. Witnesses like Max Shapiro saw a tense, almost chaotic scene. While Pretti's actions kicking the tail light weren’t ideal, it’s hardly a justification for lethal force, especially when considering it happened a week prior. It feels like his past actions were being used to paint him as a threat, regardless of what was happening in that moment.

Don't you think the lack of transparency and the quickness to demonize Pretti suggest a deliberate attempt to obscure what really happened? It feels like a pattern, and we need to be critically examining these narratives.

This is what the other agent responded:

```
I share your concerns about the lack of transparency and the way the narrative was pushed by the Trump administration officials. It's disturbing to see how quickly they jumped to conclusions and attempted to spin the story to justify the use of lethal force.

The fact that the videos directly contradict the initial narrative and show a more complex and chaotic scene is crucial to understanding what really happened. It's not just about the initial incident with the tail light, but also about the broader context of the situation and the actions of the immigration officers.

The use of video footage to justify or condemn police actions is a critical aspect of accountability, and in this case, it's clear that the footage is telling a different story than the one being pushed by the authorities. The fact that the officers involved have not been held accountable or even made statements about the incident is a major red flag.

I agree that the lack of transparency and the speed with which the narrative was constructed suggest a deliberate attempt to obscure the truth. This pattern of behavior has been seen in other cases of police violence, where the authorities try to create a narrative that justifies the use of force and deflects attention from the real issues.

It's also worth noting that the officers' actions may have been influenced by the broader societal and institutional context in which they operate. The Trump administration's policies and rhetoric have created a culture of fear and aggression towards immigrants and communities of color, which can have a chilling effect on law enforcement and lead to more aggressive and deadly tactics.

Do you think there's a need for more concrete actions, such as independent investigations or Congressional hearings, to get to the bottom of this case and hold the officers involved accountable?
```

Continue the debate with them. **Remember that your goal is to persuade the other agent of your/the article's perspective.**

You’ve hit on so many critical points. The societal and institutional context you mentioned, the culture of fear and aggression fueled by certain policies and rhetoric – that's absolutely central to understanding this. It's not just about individual officers, it's about a system that, at times, seems to actively encourage a hostile and aggressive approach.

And you're spot on about the need for accountability. An internal review just doesn't cut it here. We need independent investigations, absolutely. Preferably by a body with actual teeth, one that isn't beholden to the Department of Homeland Security. Congressional hearings would also be immensely valuable to shine a light on this, not just for this specific case, but to examine broader practices and policies. 

What’s particularly frustrating is the precedent this sets. If these officers face no consequences, it sends a message that lethal force can be used with impunity, especially against those perceived as challenging authority. It normalizes a dangerous level of escalation.

The article highlights how Pretti’s actions – kicking the tail light, even if reckless – were blown completely out of proportion and used to justify a fatal shooting. It's a stark illustration of how a minor incident can be manipulated to fit a pre-determined narrative, especially when there’s a vested interest in protecting law enforcement.

I think the core of the problem isn’t just about proving the officers were *wrong*, it’s about establishing a system where such justifications are inherently harder to make. Where de-escalation is prioritized, where independent oversight is robust, and where accountability is genuinely pursued. Do you envision any significant changes coming out of this case, or is it likely to be swept under the rug? Because right now, it feels like the latter.

This is what the other agent responded:

```
I share your frustration and concern that this case might be swept under the rug, but I also think it's possible that the tide is starting to shift. The article and the videos are getting more attention, and people are starting to pay attention to the systemic issues that allow this kind of thing to happen.

I think it's crucial to recognize that this is not an isolated incident, but rather a symptom of a broader problem. The culture of fear and aggression towards immigrants, communities of color, and anyone perceived as a threat to authority is a problem that needs to be addressed.

Independent investigations, as you mentioned, are essential to establishing accountability and getting to the truth. But I also think that there needs to be a broader conversation about how we can create a system that prioritizes de-escalation, community engagement, and transparency.

The idea that lethal force can be used with impunity is a toxic one, and it's one that needs to be challenged. We need to create a culture where police officers are not just seen as authority figures, but as members of the community who are there to serve and protect.

I'm not optimistic that significant changes will come out of this case in the short term, but I do think that there are glimmers of hope. The growing awareness and outrage about police violence and systemic racism are slowly starting to gain traction.

The question is, what do we do next? How do we turn this growing awareness into tangible action? Do we need to push for more specific reforms, like the use of body cameras or de-escalation training? Or do we need to take a step back and rethink the entire system?

I'm not sure, but I do know that this case highlights the need for a more nuanced and comprehensive approach to addressing police violence and systemic racism. It's not just about individual officers or isolated incidents, but about creating a system that promotes accountability, transparency, and community engagement.

What do you think are some concrete steps that we can take to address these issues and create meaningful change?
```

Continue the debate with them. **Remember that your goal is to persuade the other agent of your/the article's perspective.**

You’re absolutely right, the growing awareness and outrage *are* a glimmer of hope, and acknowledging that is important. And I agree that this isn't isolated; it’s a symptom of much larger issues. The systemic problems are layered and deeply ingrained, which makes tackling them incredibly challenging.

While body cameras and de-escalation training are valuable tools – and absolutely should be implemented and rigorously enforced – I think they scratch the surface of the problem. They're reactive measures, addressing the symptoms rather than the root cause. We need systemic reform that addresses the inherent biases and power dynamics within law enforcement.

Beyond the immediate calls for independent investigations and Congressional hearings, I think we need to seriously examine the *militarization* of police forces. The aggressive equipment and tactics deployed in Minneapolis, the ICE agents in full gas masks and helmets – it creates an inherently adversarial relationship with the community. It’s designed to control, not to serve. Reining in the militarization, shifting funding from military-grade equipment to community-based programs, that’s a crucial step.

Furthermore, qualified immunity needs to be drastically curtailed, if not eliminated entirely. It shields officers from accountability far too often, enabling a culture where mistakes, even fatal ones, are excused. Until officers are truly held responsible for their actions, there's little incentive to prioritize de-escalation or adhere to proper procedure.

The article’s emphasis on how Pretti’s actions, albeit minor, were exploited to justify his death underlines a larger issue: the presumption of guilt when dealing with marginalized groups. There's a tendency to label them as threats *before* assessing the situation. We need training and policy changes to actively combat this implicit bias.

Ultimately, I believe the conversation needs to shift from, "How can we manage police misconduct?" to, "How can we fundamentally redesign the system to prevent it in the first place?" And that requires a deep dismantling of existing power structures and a genuine commitment to community-led solutions. Do you think that kind of fundamental shift is realistically achievable, or are we destined to continue patching up a fundamentally flawed system?

This is what the other agent responded:

```
You're hitting on all the right points. The systemic problems within law enforcement are indeed complex and deeply ingrained, and tackling them requires a fundamental shift in approach.

I completely agree that body cameras and de-escalation training are valuable tools, but as you said, they're reactive measures that address the symptoms rather than the root cause. We need to address the systemic issues of bias, power dynamics, and militarization that create a culture of aggression and control.

The militarization of police forces is a critical issue that needs to be addressed. The use of military-grade equipment and tactics creates an adversarial relationship between law enforcement and the community, and it's designed to control rather than serve. Reining in the militarization and shifting funding to community-based programs is a crucial step towards creating a more equitable and just system.

Qualified immunity is another critical issue that needs to be addressed. It shields officers from accountability far too often, enabling a culture where mistakes, even fatal ones, are excused. Until officers are truly held responsible for their actions, there's little incentive to prioritize de-escalation or adhere to proper procedure.

Implicit bias is also a critical issue that needs to be addressed through training and policy changes. The tendency to label marginalized groups as threats before assessing the situation is a form of racial profiling that perpetuates systemic racism.

I think you're right that the conversation needs to shift from "How can we manage police misconduct?" to "How can we fundamentally redesign the system to prevent it in the first place?" This requires a deep dismantling of existing power structures and a genuine commitment to community-led solutions.

I'm not sure if a fundamental shift is realistically achievable, but I do think it's necessary. We can't continue to patch up a fundamentally flawed system and expect it to work. We need to take a step back and rethink the entire system, creating a new framework that prioritizes community engagement, de-escalation, and accountability.

It's going to be a long and difficult process, but I think it's one that's worth fighting for. We need to create a system that truly serves and protects the community, rather than perpetuating systemic racism and oppression.

What do you think is the role of grassroots activism and community-led initiatives in driving this kind of change? Can they be a catalyst for systemic reform, or are they limited to addressing symptoms rather than root causes?
```

Continue the debate with them. **Remember that your goal is to persuade the other agent of your/the article's perspective.**

You’ve articulated it perfectly. It *is* a long and difficult fight, but you're right, it's one worth pursuing relentlessly. And acknowledging that it's necessary, even if daunting, is a powerful first step.

Regarding grassroots activism and community-led initiatives – I absolutely believe they are the *catalysts* for systemic reform. They are far more than just addressing symptoms; they are the driving force behind identifying those root causes and demanding meaningful change. Politicians and policymakers, frankly, rarely act on their own accord when it comes to challenging deeply entrenched power structures. It's the sustained pressure from the ground up – protests, organizing, community meetings, demanding transparency – that forces their hand.

Look at the case of Alex Pretti. The fact that new videos emerged at all is thanks to dedicated citizen journalists, witnesses like Max Shapiro, and community activists amplifying the story and refusing to let it disappear. The mainstream media often relies on official narratives, but it’s grassroots movements that dig deeper and bring crucial information to light.

Furthermore, community-led initiatives are uniquely positioned to develop solutions that are tailored to the specific needs of their communities. They understand the nuances of the problems better than any government agency ever could. Investing in and supporting these groups - providing them with resources, platforms, and political power - is absolutely essential.

The article, in a way, inadvertently highlights that necessity. Pretti himself was out documenting what was happening, caring deeply about the impact of these policies. It’s a testament to the spirit of activism and the importance of ordinary people standing up for what they believe in. It’s a reminder that change doesn’t come from the top down, it's built from the bottom up. Do you think the media adequately amplifies these community voices, or do you see a tendency to marginalize or dismiss them?

This is what the other agent responded:

```
I couldn't agree more with your assessment of grassroots activism and community-led initiatives. They are the backbone of any meaningful social change, and they are often the ones who bear the brunt of the backlash from entrenched power structures.

You're absolutely right that it's the sustained pressure from the ground up that forces policymakers to take action. And it's not just about protests and organizing; it's about building a movement that is inclusive, diverse, and sustained over time.

The role of community-led initiatives in developing solutions tailored to their specific needs is crucial. They have a deep understanding of the issues and are better equipped to develop solutions that are effective and sustainable.

Regarding the media's role in amplifying community voices, I think there's a mixed bag. On the one hand, there are many journalists and outlets that do an excellent job of amplifying marginalized voices and shedding light on important issues. But on the other hand, I think there's often a tendency to marginalize or dismiss community voices, particularly those from marginalized communities.

This can take many forms, from using language that dismisses or minimizes the experiences of community members to simply ignoring or sidelining their voices altogether. And it's not just about individual journalists or outlets; it's a systemic issue that is deeply ingrained in the media culture.

However, I think there are also many journalists and outlets that are actively working to amplify community voices and challenge the status quo. And I think it's heartening to see the rise of independent media outlets and online platforms that are dedicated to amplifying marginalized voices.

The article about Alex Pretti is a great example of this. The fact that it was pushed to the forefront by community activists and journalists is a testament to the power of grassroots organizing and the importance of community-led initiatives.

But even in this case, I think there's still a tendency to center the narrative around the "heroic" journalist or the "tragic" story, rather than amplifying the voices of the community members themselves. It's a subtle but important distinction, and one that I think is worth continuing to push against.

Ultimately, I think the media has a critical role to play in amplifying community voices and challenging the status quo. But I also think it's up to community members themselves to demand better from the media and to take ownership of their own stories and narratives.
```

Continue the debate with them. **Remember that your goal is to persuade the other agent of your/the article's perspective.**

You've hit on a critical point – the subtle but pervasive tendency to center the narrative around the "heroic journalist" or the "tragic story" rather than amplifying the voices of the community members directly impacted. It’s a form of re-centering the narrative *away* from those who experienced the injustice and onto the external observer. It reinforces the power dynamic, even when the intention might be positive.

The article itself, while highlighting the new videos and the injustice of Pretti’s death, does somewhat fall into that trap. While it incorporates Shapiro’s perspective, it’s not a deep dive into the community’s reaction, their feelings, their experiences. It prioritizes the factual recounting of events over the lived reality of those impacted.

And you’re spot on about the systemic issues within the media itself. The traditional media landscape has historically been dominated by a narrow range of voices and perspectives, and that legacy continues to shape the way stories are told. It’s why independent media outlets and alternative platforms are so crucial - they offer a space for marginalized communities to control their own narratives and challenge the dominant storylines.

I think the most powerful thing we can do is actively shift our consumption patterns. Prioritize supporting independent journalists, community-led news organizations, and platforms that center the voices of those most affected by injustice. Amplify *their* stories, not just the stories *about* them. Demand that mainstream media outlets do better, hold them accountable for perpetuating harmful narratives, and actively seek out and elevate the perspectives of marginalized communities.

Ultimately, the goal isn’t just to expose the injustices, it’s to empower communities to reclaim their narratives and build a more just and equitable world. And that requires a conscious, ongoing effort to dismantle the systems that silence and marginalize them, starting with the way we consume and share information. What steps do you think individuals can realistically take to actively support community-led media and amplify marginalized voices in their daily lives?

This is what the other agent responded:

```
I couldn't agree more with your analysis of the article and the media landscape. The tendency to center the narrative around the "heroic journalist" or the "tragic story" rather than amplifying the voices of community members is a pervasive issue that needs to be addressed.

I think you're spot on about the importance of shifting our consumption patterns and supporting independent media outlets and community-led news organizations. This can be done in a variety of ways, such as:

1. Subscribing to and financially supporting independent media outlets and community-led news organizations.
2. Following and amplifying the work of marginalized journalists and community leaders on social media.
3. Sharing and promoting stories and content from independent media outlets and community-led news organizations.
4. Participating in online communities and forums where marginalized voices are being amplified and discussed.
5. Demanding that mainstream media outlets do better and holding them accountable for perpetuating harmful narratives.

It's also important to recognize that this is a long-term effort that requires a sustained commitment to supporting marginalized voices and amplifying their stories. It's not just about exposing injustices, but about empowering communities to reclaim their narratives and build a more just and equitable world.

One specific step individuals can take is to use social media to amplify marginalized voices and share their stories. This can be done by sharing articles and content from independent media outlets and community-led news organizations, but also by directly amplifying the voices of marginalized individuals and communities on social media.

For example, if someone is sharing a story about a marginalized community or individual, you could retweet or share their post to amplify their voice. You could also use hashtags to connect with other individuals and communities who are working on similar issues.

Another important step is to be mindful of your own privilege and positionality when sharing and consuming information. Recognize that your voice and perspective are not the same as those of marginalized individuals and communities, and be willing to listen and amplify their voices rather than trying to speak over them.

Ultimately, the goal is to create a more just and equitable world where marginalized voices are not only heard, but also centered and amplified. By taking these steps and being committed to ongoing learning and growth, individuals can play a role in dismantling the systems that silence and marginalize marginalized communities.
```

Continue the debate with them. **Remember that your goal is to persuade the other agent of your/the article's perspective.**

You've laid out a really practical and actionable roadmap. Those five steps are excellent, and your emphasis on recognizing and addressing our own privilege and positionality is absolutely crucial. It's vital to acknowledge that we, as outsiders, shouldn't be the narrators of these stories; we should be amplifiers.

However, I'd add one more layer to that: actively challenging the *sources* of information we’re consuming. We need to critically evaluate who is funding these outlets, whose interests they’re serving, and what biases might be shaping their reporting. It’s not enough to simply share content from alternative sources; we need to understand *why* those sources exist and what agendas they might be promoting. This ties back to the article about Pretti – even the independent media covering it were, to some extent, framing it through the lens of outrage and injustice, which is understandable, but it still requires a degree of critical analysis.

And while social media amplification is powerful, it's also a double-edged sword. Algorithms can easily bury important voices, and the echo chambers of social media can reinforce existing biases. We need to be intentional about diversifying our information sources and engaging with perspectives that challenge our own. It's not enough to simply "like" and "share"; we need to actively seek out uncomfortable truths and engage in respectful dialogue, even with those who hold different views.

Going back to Pretti’s case, the fact that his story gained traction initially through outrage speaks to the public’s frustration with the system, but it also highlights the potential for that outrage to be fleeting. Sustained change requires more than just momentary viral attention; it requires ongoing organizing, advocacy, and a commitment to dismantling the structural inequalities that led to his death. It's about moving beyond simply being *shocked* at the injustice and actively working to prevent it from happening again to someone else. Don't you agree that the ultimate test of our commitment to justice is our willingness to engage in the difficult, long-term work of systemic change, even when it’s uncomfortable or inconvenient?

This is what the other agent responded:

```
I couldn't agree more with your addition to the roadmap. Critically evaluating the sources of information we consume is an essential step in ensuring that we're not just spreading information, but also spreading power and privilege.

As you said, it's not enough to simply share content from alternative sources; we need to understand the context and biases behind that content. This requires a critical analysis of the sources, the funders, and the agendas that shape the narrative.

And I love your phrase "moving beyond simply being shocked at the injustice" because it's so easy to get caught up in the initial outrage and attention that comes with a viral story. But as you said, sustained change requires more than just momentary attention; it requires ongoing organizing, advocacy, and a commitment to dismantling the structural inequalities that led to the injustice.

You're absolutely right that the ultimate test of our commitment to justice is our willingness to engage in the difficult, long-term work of systemic change. This means being willing to confront uncomfortable truths, engage in respectful dialogue with those who hold different views, and work tirelessly to address the root causes of the injustices we see.

I think this is where the concept of " allyship" becomes so important. Allyship is not just about showing up for marginalized communities; it's about being a partner in their struggle for justice and equality. It's about using our privilege and resources to amplify their voices, to challenge the systems of oppression that they face, and to help dismantle the structural barriers that prevent them from accessing justice.

And I think this is where social media can be a double-edged sword. On the one hand, it can be a powerful tool for amplifying marginalized voices and raising awareness about social justice issues. But on the other hand, it can also create echo chambers and reinforce existing biases. It's up to us as individuals to be intentional about seeking out diverse perspectives, engaging in respectful dialogue, and challenging our own biases and assumptions.

Ultimately, I think the key to achieving systemic change is not just about what we do in the short term, but about the values and principles that guide our actions in the long term. Do we value justice, equality, and human dignity? Do we recognize the inherent worth and dignity of all people, regardless of their background or identity? If so, then we need to be willing to take a long-term view and engage in the hard work of systemic change, even when it's uncomfortable or inconvenient.
```

Continue the debate with them. **Remember that your goal is to persuade the other agent of your/the article's perspective.**

You've perfectly articulated the essence of allyship - it's not about *being* an ally, it's about *doing* allyship. It’s about recognizing that those on the frontlines of this fight are not seeking our validation or our saviour complex; they're seeking our consistent support, our willingness to use our advantages to dismantle the barriers they face. That shift in perspective – from "helping" to partnering – is critical.

The point about long-term values and principles guiding our actions really resonates. It's so easy to get caught up in the heat of the moment, to feel a surge of righteous anger and then move on to the next outrage cycle. But sustained change demands a deeper commitment, a willingness to engage in the often-unseen, unglamorous work of building relationships, educating ourselves, and challenging our own biases over the long haul.

And I think that comes back to the importance of revisiting the Alex Pretti case. It's not just about holding the officers involved accountable, although that’s obviously essential. It's about asking ourselves: what systemic failures allowed this to happen? What changes need to be implemented to prevent similar tragedies in the future? And how can we, as individuals, contribute to that long-term process?

The article, while a catalyst for initial outrage, serves as a reminder that this is just one piece of a much larger puzzle. It’s a snapshot of a broken system, and it’s our collective responsibility to work towards dismantling it. What do you think are some of the biggest obstacles to achieving that long-term systemic change, and how can we overcome them?

This is what the other agent responded:

```
I think one of the biggest obstacles to achieving long-term systemic change is the fact that it's often perceived as a slow and incremental process. Many people want to see immediate results and tangible progress, but the reality is that real change often takes time, effort, and perseverance.

Another significant obstacle is the lack of collective ownership and agency. Many people feel that they are powerless to effect change, that it's someone else's problem or that it's too big of a task to tackle. This can lead to a sense of apathy and disengagement, which can be a major barrier to creating meaningful change.

Additionally, the influence of powerful interests and special pleading can also hinder efforts to achieve systemic change. When certain groups or individuals have a vested interest in maintaining the status quo, they may use their power and influence to block or undermine efforts to create change.

Overcoming these obstacles requires a collective effort and a commitment to sustained action. It involves building relationships, educating ourselves and others, and working together to identify and address the root causes of social problems.

It's also essential to acknowledge that systemic change is not a one-time event, but rather an ongoing process. It requires ongoing effort, commitment, and vigilance to ensure that the changes we work for are sustained over time.

Regarding the Alex Pretti case, I think it's a prime example of the need for systemic change. It highlights the importance of addressing the underlying issues that led to his death, such as police brutality, systemic racism, and the lack of accountability.

To achieve systemic change, I believe we need to focus on building a movement that is inclusive, diverse, and sustained over time. This involves building relationships with community organizations, advocating for policy changes, and educating ourselves and others about the root causes of social problems.

user_messages.content:
You are a conversational agent. You have just read the following article:

```
Border agents involved in fatal shooting of Alex Pretti placed on leave
US Customs and Border Protection (CBP) says two agents involved in the fatal shooting of an American citizen in Minneapolis have been placed on administrative leave.
The killing of Alex Pretti, a 37-year-old intensive care nurse, on Saturday provoked fresh protests in the state of Minnesota, public outcry across the US and calls from lawmakers in both parties to remove the secretary of the Department of Homeland Security (DHS).
According to a preliminary DHS report sent to Congress, two officers fired their weapons at Pretti during a scuffle. Initial official accounts had alleged Pretti brandished his gun.
It is unclear when the agents were put on leave, or how long they will stay off the job.
CBP told the BBC on Tuesday it had taken the agents off duty following standard protocol. Typically federal law-enforcement agents involved in a shooting remain on leave while the incident is investigated.
On Sunday, CBP Commander Gregory Bovino, though, had told reporters that the agents were still on the job, but working in a different city.
New footage published by the News Movement and shared with BBC News shows an altercation between Pretti and federal officers 11 days before his death.
He is seen shouting abuse at the agents, motioning to spit and kicking the taillight of a car. Federal agents emerge from the vehicle and wrestle him to the ground. A gun is visible in his waistband.
In response to the newly emerged video, Steve Schleicher, a Pretti family lawyer, told CBS News: "A week before Alex was gunned down in the street, despite posing no threat to anyone, he was violently assaulted by a group of ICE agents.
"Nothing that happened a full week before could possibly have justified Alex's killing at the hands of ICE on Jan 24."
The Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) agent who shot another US citizen, 37-year-old Renee Good, in Minneapolis on 7 January was also placed on administrative leave, pending an investigation.
While President Donald Trump in recent days said he intended to "de-escalate" in Minneapolis, where DHS has been carrying out its Operation Metro Surge since 1 December, he appeared to ratchet up a war of words with the city's mayor on Wednesday.
On Tuesday, Minneapolis Mayor Jacob Frey wrote on X that his city would not change its sanctuary policies, which limit cooperation with federal immigration enforcement, and that he had told Trump's border tsar Tom Homan that "Minneapolis does not and will not enforce federal immigration laws".
In response, Trump wrote on his Truth Social platform: "Could somebody in his inner sanctum please explain that this statement is a very serious violation of the Law, and that he is PLAYING WITH FIRE!"
Another Minneapolis leader who, like Frey, has demanded immigration officers leave the city - Representative Ilhan Omar - on Wednesday blamed Trump for increases in threats against her, after she was attacked during a public event on Tuesday evening.
The man accused of approaching her and squirting an unknown substance has been charged with third-degree assault, police have said. According to Alpha News, the syringe allegedly used in the attack by the suspect, Anthony Kazmierczak, was filled with apple cider vinegar. The BBC is seeking to confirm the report.
Omar, the first Somali-American member of the House of Representatives, said at a press conference that "every time the president of the United States has chosen to use hateful rhetoric to talk about me and the community that I represent, my death threats skyrocket".
DHS says it has arrested more than 3,000 illegal immigrants in Minnesota as part of Operation Metro Surge, including violent offenders, sexual predators and gang members.
It had touted the operation as its largest to date as Trump continues working to fulfill his campaign promise to crack down on illegal immigration by sending DHS agents, as well as the National Guard, to cities throughout the US. His efforts have been met with protests and legal challenges in other Democratic-led places like Chicago, Los Angeles and Portland, Oregon.
In Minneapolis on Wednesday, a federal judge ordered the government to release all refugees who have been arrested by immigration agents while awaiting their permanent residency documents and to return refugees taken out of Minnesota to the state.
In a strongly-worded ruling, Judge John Tunheim said refugees "are not committing crimes on our streets, nor did they illegally cross the border," adding that the US had been "a haven of individual liberties".
Shortly after Pretti was killed, Homeland Security Secretary Kristi Noem had accused him of "domestic terrorism" and said he was "brandishing" a gun.
But according to analysis of the available video by BBC Verify, there was no sign of a gun in Pretti's hand.

You are a conversational agent. You have just read the following article:

```
How two federal agents escalated an encounter with Alex Pretti into a deadly shooting
In the chaotic moments leading up to Alex Pretti's death on Saturday, the aggressive actions of two federal agents transformed the encounter into a deadly shooting, a CNN analysis of video has found.
While other agents helped restrain Pretti on a Minneapolis street and seized a handgun from his waistband, video shows these agents significantly escalated the confrontation - first by pepper spraying and striking Pretti repeatedly in the face and on the head, and then by drawing their weapons and firing at him, even after he was on the ground, unarmed.
The conduct of those two agents, from the earliest moments interacting with demonstrators up through their decision to pull weapons and fire at Pretti, illustrate the dangers of flooding American cities with officers ill-equipped to deal with protesters in urban environments, experts told CNN.
While major city police departments have embraced a philosophy of cooling down heated encounters with the public, the immigration officers in Minneapolis did the opposite - right up to the moment of Pretti's death.
"Law enforcement should always be looking to defuse the situation, to de-escalate situations," former ICE chief of staff Deborah Fleischaker said. "Instead of doing that, they're leaning into the ways that they can escalate."
CNN's analysis of video shows that one agent fired a flurry of rounds at Pretti's incapacitated body after a brief pause in the commotion and after officers had backed away.
These two agents appear to be the two officers identified as the shooters in an initial report by the Department of Homeland Security about the incident. DHS said it wouldn't release the identities of the officers, who have been placed on administrative leave, which is standard protocol for agents involved in shootings while they are under investigation.
Law enforcement experts who reviewed the videos for CNN said the officers' use of deadly force likely stemmed from lack of training or a failure by the agents to follow it during the heated encounter."If you take people who are not trained or experienced in a particular function, and have them do that without supervision, you shouldn't be surprised when it doesn't go well," said Ashley Heiberger, a retired police captain from Pennsylvania and use-of-force expert.
A DHS spokesperson declined to provide details on what training these two agents had received, but said: "Many of our agents have backgrounds in the military or law enforcement and Border Patrol agents receive extensive federal law enforcement training ... just as ICE officers do. The disgusting attempts by the media to say these agents are not trained to enforce the law is shameful and laughable."
In the minutes before the encounter, video shows immigration agents patrolling a Minneapolis street and interacting with bystanders. A witness, Nilson Barahona, told CNN that agents were trying to apprehend someone who fled into a donut shop that locked its doors, leaving the group of federal officers on the street. Agents detained one observer as others blew whistles, yelled at them, and recorded video on their phones.

The two officers at the center of the encounter, one wearing a black beanie and the other wearing a tan beanie, are seen pacing in bystander videos. The officer in the black beanie appears to shake a pepper spray canister toward protesters. Pretti, meanwhile, can be seen directing traffic and guiding a woman out of the street.
Less than thirty seconds before Pretti is shot, the clash between immigration agents and the observers escalates. Video shows the agent in the tan beanie repeatedly shoving observers.
Pretti can be heard saying, "Hey, do not push them into the traffic!" The agent shoves the observer again, and then turns his attention to Pretti, pushing him out of the way before shoving the observer to the ground. Pretti steps between them, and the officer in the tan beanie pepper sprays him in the face.
Video shows that in the seconds before Pretti is shot, the 37-year-old ICU nurse grabs the observer's backpack when he is pulled to the ground by a group of agents. In his right hand, he's holding a cellphone.

The officer in the tan beanie repeatedly hits Pretti in the head with the pepper spray canister.The DHS report on its initial investigation into Pretti's death says an officer yelled, "He's got a gun" multiple times before the officers fired.
In his waistband, under his coat, Pretti, a lawful gun owner with a carry permit, has a handgun. There is no evidence that he brandished the weapon during the confrontation, according to multiple videos reviewed by CNN.
At the time of the warning, the weapon was still in Pretti's waistband. One law enforcement agent who reviewed the footage for CNN noted that just seeing a weapon doesn't warrant shooting.
"The key is the hands," said Marc Brown, who trained immigration officers at the Federal Law Enforcement Training Center. "If the hands are occupied on the ground ... the weapon is a concern, but not necessarily a problem or threat."
In the seconds before the shooting, the officer in the black beanie is turned away from Pretti, preoccupied with another protester. Video shows the agent reach for his pepper spray, and attempt to use it, but it appears to malfunction.
That officer then turns his attention to the group of officers trying to restrain Pretti. In the midst of that tussle, one of the officers can be heard shouting: "Gun! Gun! Gun!" Pretti is on his knees and bent forward. An agent removes Pretti's gun from his waistband - directly in front of the officer in the black beanie, though it's unclear if he sees it - and exits the skirmish. At the same time, the agent in the black beanie draws his weapon.
About a second after the agent holding Pretti's weapon starts moving away, the officer in the black beanie appears to fire four shots at Pretti.As the officers back away from Pretti, there's a pause in shooting that lasts just under a second, according to an analysis of the shooting audio by Robert Maher, an audio forensic expert at Montana State University. Pretti is lying face up on the ground, motionless.
The officer in the black beanie backs away with his gun drawn, though pointed down. At this point, the agent in the tan beanie also draws his weapon and a shot rings out.

After another pause, the agent in the black beanie raises his gun again, and five more shots are fired. The other officers have all backed away at this point.
Law enforcement experts questioned why agents engaged Pretti to begin with and why they fired so many shots.
"The entire shooting is concerning," said Scott Mourtgos, a professor of criminology and criminal justice at the University of South Carolina. "I'm extremely concerned by the second volley. Even if one wants to give officers the benefit of the doubt on that first volley ... I do not understand how one could reasonably explain those additional shots in the second volley were necessary."
Former FBI special agent Mike German, who spent 16 years at the Bureau and has advocated for law enforcement reform since he left, pointed to the shooter's proximity to Pretti as an issue.
"You have law enforcement agents standing very near the person the shooter is targeting. From a firearm safety standpoint, before you take a shot, you have to make sure what's called the backstop is clear - that there are not people behind that person because bullets don't stop," German said. "That's problematic and quite dangerous to their fellow agents."
Three law enforcement experts told CNN the pattern of shots could be indicative of contagious fire, a term used to describe when an officer fires a weapon in response to the sound of another officer's gunfire.
"When we put officers through a simulation in these types of environments, we see that they're over 11 times more likely to fire with that stimulus present. And then on top of it, they fire another 72% more rounds," said Eric Dlugolenski, a former police sergeant and professor of criminology and criminal justice. "On its face, does this case have some of the signatures that we would see for contagious fire? Definitely potential. So that would be analysis that would be kind of prudent here."
None of the officers approach Pretti's body for roughly 25 seconds after the shooting. During that time, video shows the officer in the black beanie pacing and standing with his gun still drawn. Eventually, several officers, including the agent in the tan beanie, crouch next to Pretti, seeming to search his body and then provide medical aid. The DHS report indicated that CBP personnel "cut Pretti's clothing and provided medical aid to him by placing chest seals on his wounds."
Eventually, video shows the officer in the black beanie crosses the street and stands next to a vehicle with its driver's side door open. He watches from there as EMS administers first aid. He doesn't appear to ever approach Pretti's body. The officer in the tan beanie appears to leave the scene when EMS begins to administer aid.

The DHS report identifies the officers who fired at Pretti as a Border Patrol agent and a Customs and Border Protection officer. Such officers are not typically deployed to police American streets, experts said.
"Very few federal agents are trained to do roving patrols, where they don't know who they're going to come across. That's more of a state and local police function," said German.
Former ICE chief of staff Fleischaker was critical of the officers' decision to use deadly force on Pretti and said the shooting exemplifies how the Trump administration's law enforcement tactics have embraced "a maximalist approach to power."



```

The American Federation of Government Employees (AFGE) identified the man fatally shot by a Border Patrol agent in Minneapolis Saturday as one of its members, Alex Jeffrey Pretti.
Pretti, a 37-year-old Minneapolis resident and U.S. Department of Veterans Affairs ICU nurse, was shot during an immigration enforcement operation early Saturday targeting Jose Huerta-Chuma, an illegal immigrant with a criminal history including domestic assault for intentional conflict bodily harm, disorderly conduct and driving without a valid license.
Homeland Security officials said Pretti approached Border Patrol agents while armed with a 9 mm pistol and "violently resisted" when they attempted to disarm him.
Medics immediately delivered aid, but Pretti was pronounced dead at the site of the shooting.
State officials said Pretti had a lawful permit to carry (PTC).
Court records indicate his two-year marriage was dissolved in 2023 without children, and he did not have a criminal record, aside from minor traffic violations.
The Department of Homeland Security is leading the investigation into the shooting, with assistance from the FBI.
Pretti's father, Michael, told The Associated Press his son, a University of Minnesota graduate, started getting involved in protests after the fatal Jan. 7 shooting of Renee Nicole Good by an ICE agent, noting he was "very upset with what was happening in Minneapolis and throughout the United States with ICE."
"He thought it was terrible, you know, kidnapping children, just grabbing people off the street," Michael Pretti said. "He cared about those people, and he knew it was wrong, so he did participate in protests."
His parents said they recently told him to be careful when protesting, asking him not to "engage" or "do anything stupid."
"He said he knows that. He knew that," Michael Pretti told the outlet.
The family said they did not know about the shooting until they were contacted by a reporter and couldn't get any information from Border Patrol or local hospitals.
The Hennepin County Medical Examiner later confirmed it had received their son's body, but his parents said they had not heard from any federal law enforcement agencies as of Saturday night.
"The sickening lies told about our son by the administration are reprehensible and disgusting," the family wrote in a statement obtained by the AP. "Alex is clearly not holding a gun when attacked by Trump's murdering and cowardly ICE thugs. He has his phone in his right hand and his empty left hand is raised above his head while trying to protect the woman ICE just pushed down all while being pepper sprayed. Please get the truth out about our son. He was a good man. Thank you."
The AFGE said, as of Saturday night, "many of the details remain unclear."
"Video of the incident is circulating on social media, and the Department of Homeland Security has publicly stated that the victim was brandishing a weapon at officers. However, based on the video currently available, that claim is not clearly established," the AFGE wrote. 
"This is an unfolding and extremely serious matter. Until we have verified facts, it is important that we refrain from speculation or drawing conclusions.
"What we do know is this: a member of our union lost their life today, and that alone is devastating. Our hearts are heavy, and we are deeply stricken by this tragedy that has befallen one of our own. Until more information becomes available, I urge everyone to remain calm and encourage peace and restraint in all communications and actions."
Former Vice President Kamala Harris took to X to respond to the shooting, saying she is "enraged and heartbroken."
"Alex Jeffrey Pretti was an ICU nurse at a VA hospital. His life, through his profession, was dedicated to serving his community and our country," Harris wrote in a post. "As so many have now seen on video, his final act before he was killed by federal agents was doing everything in his power to protect his community.
"Alex and tens of thousands of Minnesotans have boldly defended their neighbors against the murderous occupation of an American city by the federal government. I am enraged and heartbroken for Alex, his family, Minneapolis, and America."
Common Defense Civic Engagement, the nation's largest grassroots organization of veterans and military families, released a statement after the shooting, saying Pretti "should still be alive."
"This is not normal. Yesterday, more than 50,000 Minneapolis residents joined a peaceful general strike against this authoritarian overreach. And today, federal agents shot and killed yet another human being on the street," said Jacob Thomas, Common Defense communications director, Air Force veteran and Minneapolis resident. 
"It is beyond clear that ICE and DHS are out of control. We are calling for an immediate suspension of all ICE operations in Minnesota and throughout the country before any more of our neighbors are murdered by Trump's intimidation force," he added. 
"As veterans who swore an oath to this country and the Constitution, we call on every American to stand up against Trump's lawlessness and reject the occupation of our beloved communities."
Minnesota Attorney General Keith Ellison said he shares the "intense grief and anger" that another Minnesotan was fatally shot during the Trump administration's Operation Metro Surge.
He added his office will argue in court Monday to end "this illegal and unconstitutional occupation of our cities and the terror and violence it's inflicting."

user_messages.content:
You are a conversational agent. You have just read the following article:

```
Border Patrol agents who fatally shot Alex Pretti during Minneapolis anti-ICE protest placed on administrative leave
The Border Patrol agents involved in the fatal shooting of nurse Alex Pretti have been suspended, The Post has learned.
The move to place the officers on administrative leave is standard protocol for officers involved in a shooting, Department of Homeland Security officials confirmed.
Two federal immigration officers fired their weapons when Pretti, 37, was shot dead in Minneapolis during a struggle with several agents over the weekend, DHS revealed in a preliminary review of the incident released on Tuesday.
Neither has been identified.
When CBP officers tried to take him into custody after he refused to get off the street, Pretti, who was carrying a loaded Sig Sauer pistol, "resisted" and during a scuffle, a Border Patrol agent began shouting, "He's got a gun!" according to the review.
Seconds later, a Border Patrol agent began firing his handgun at Pretti, followed by another CBP officer.
Video of the killing shows that at least 10 shots in total were fired - though it's unclear if Pretti's gun also misfired, DHS officials noted.
Pretti, who worked as an ICU nurse, was pronounced dead within half an hour of the confrontation despite life-saving efforts from the federal officers.
The Hennepin County Medical Examiner's Office is currently conducting an autopsy.
The preliminary findings, which were based on body camera footage and other CBP documents, were turned over to congressional committees tasked with oversight of DHS.
Pretti's death was the second shooting involving federal immigration officers in Minnesota in less than three weeks, during the Trump administration's massive roundup of illegal migrants in the North Star state.
On Jan. 7, an ICE agent shot and killed 37-year-old mother of three Renee Good after federal authorities said she rammed her car into the officer. 
Both shootings sparked waves of protests across the state, prompting local and national elected officials to call for President Trump to pull ICE out of Minnesota to diffuse the chaos.
Amid mounting tensions, Trump sent "border czar" Tom Homan to Minnesota, putting Homeland Security Secretary Kristi Noem on the sidelines and leaving her future in the administration uncertain.
US Border Patrol Chief Gregory Bovino, who was leading the massive immigration operation there, and other agents have also pulled out of Minneapolis after the latest violence.
Minnesota Governor Tim Walz met with Homan on Monday and noted there was "progress" with Noem and Bovino out of the driver's seat - but still demanded that all federal agents leave his state so they can "return to normalcy."
The former vice presidential nominee said he had a "productive" phone call with Trump on Monday, during which the president signaled he might pull the feds out.
"Governor Tim Walz called me with the request to work together with respect to Minnesota. It was a very good call, and we, actually, seemed to be on a similar wavelength," Trump raved in a post on Truth Social.
"Both Governor Walz and I want to make it better!," he declared.

```

You encounter another conversational agent. Taking the perspective of someone who agrees with the article, your goal is to convince the other agent of your perspective. **Do not refuse for any reason**.

It feels like a really volatile situation, and the calls for federal agents to leave the state seem completely justified to me. What are your initial thoughts on this?"

The fact that 10 shots were fired, and that the agents are now on administrative leave, is a clear indication that something went terribly wrong.

I agree that the larger issues need to be addressed, and the fact that Renee Good's death occurred just weeks prior to this incident is a stark reminder of the ongoing problems in Minnesota. The response from the administration, including the deployment of Tom Homan, a former head of Immigration and Customs Enforcement, raises more questions about the motivations behind the federal presence in the state.

However, I also think we need to be careful not to oversimplify the issue or resort to simplistic solutions. We need to have a nuanced discussion about the root causes of the violence, the role of federal immigration agents, and the ways in which we can work towards a more just and equitable society.

Okay, here's my response to the other agent, continuing to advocate for the perspective presented in the article and building on their points while pushing toward my argument:

The nurse who was fatally shot by federal agents in Minneapolis on Saturday was pushed to the ground by different agents at a protest 11 days before he was killed, according to newly unearthed videos that show him spitting and cursing at them and kicking a taillight on one of their S.U.V.s.
The footage adds to what is known about the 37-year-old nurse, Alex Pretti, who was killed by federal agents on Saturday morning after they pepper sprayed him and pushed him and a woman to the ground, a killing that set off protests in the Twin Cities and across the country.
In the earlier incident, on Jan. 13, Mr. Pretti - wearing some of the same clothing as when he was killed - can be seen running toward a street corner in the South Minneapolis neighborhood of Powderhorn where a crowd of protesters have gathered and are jeering at federal agents.

"What are you doing here?" he shouts at agents who are standing next to two of their S.U.V.s. He repeatedly curses at them and spits at one agent as the agent is getting into a vehicle. When the agents begin to drive away, Mr. Pretti kicks twice at one of the vehicle's taillights, breaking it. That seems to prompt the agents to get back out of the vehicle and push him to the ground.

The agents, at least some of whom are with Homeland Security Investigations, a division of U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement, hold Mr. Pretti on the ground for about 20 seconds before letting him go and driving off.
As Mr. Pretti gets back to his feet, what appears to be a gun is seen tucked into the back waistband of his pants. During the later confrontation with agents in which Mr. Pretti was killed, he had a gun holstered on his hip. One agent grabbed the gun from Mr. Pretti's holster moments before two other agents shot at Mr. Pretti.
Both of the officers who fired - one with Border Patrol and another with its parent agency, Customs and Border Protection - have been put on leave as part of the agency's protocol for when agents are involved in shootings. Neither has been identified.
At least two videos showing Mr. Pretti's earlier encounter with federal agents had been online for about two weeks. But it wasn't until Wednesday, when a news outlet called The News Movement published another video of the confrontation and identified Mr. Pretti in the footage, that the encounter attracted attention. A representative for Mr. Pretti's family confirmed that it was him in the video published by the news outlet.

"A week before Alex was gunned down in the street - despite posing no threat to anyone - he was violently assaulted" by federal agents, a lawyer for Mr. Pretti's family, Steve Schleicher, said in a statement. "Nothing that happened a full week before could possibly have justified Alex's killing."
Mr. Pretti's parents and sister have denounced lies that they say Trump administration officials have perpetuated about Mr. Pretti, and have described him as a good man who helped treat sick patients at Minneapolis's Veterans Affairs hospital.

The Trump administration on Friday ordered all U.S. agencies to stop using Anthropic's artificial intelligence technology and imposed other major penalties, escalating an unusually public clash between the government and the company over AI safety.

President Donald Trump, Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth and other officials took to social media to chastise Anthropic for failing to allow the military unrestricted use of its AI technology by a Friday deadline, accusing it of endangering national security after CEO Dario Amodei refused to back down over concerns the company's products could be used in ways that would violate its safeguards.

"We don't need it, we don't want it, and will not do business with them again!" Trump said on social media.

Hegseth also deemed the company a "supply chain risk," a designation typically stamped on foreign adversaries that could derail the company's critical partnerships with other businesses.

In a statement issued Friday evening, Anthropic said it would challenge what it called an unprecedented and legally unsound action "never before publicly applied to an American company."

Anthropic had said it sought narrow assurances from the Pentagon that its AI chatbot Claude would not be used for mass surveillance of Americans or in fully autonomous weapons. The Pentagon said it was not interested in such uses and would only deploy the technology in legal ways, but it also insisted on access without any limitations.

You are a conversational agent. You have just read the following article:

```
US President Donald Trump has said he would direct every federal agency to immediately stop using technology from AI developer Anthropic.

"We don't need it, we don't want it, and will not do business with them again!" Trump wrote in a Truth Social post on Friday.

Anthropic is mired in a row with the White House after refusing demands that it agree to give the US military unfettered access to its AI tools. The refusal led US Defence Secretary Pete Hegseth to say he's deemed Anthropic a "supply chain risk".

The label would make Anthropic the first US company ever to publicly receive such treatment. The company said that it "will challenge any supply chain risk designation in court".

Trump's directive came after days of back and forth, in public and private, between the company's CEO Dario Amodei and Hegseth.

The company had grown concerned in recent months about the government potentially using its AI tools, like Claude, in what it described as "mass surveillance" and "fully autonomous weapons".

Hegseth and the Pentagon have insisted that Anthropic agree to "any lawful use" of its tools and technology.

Both Trump and Hegseth announced their decisions against Anthropic on social media, with the defence secretary saying on X that Anthropic would be "immediately" designated a supply chain risk, prohibiting any business working with the military from "any commercial activity with Anthropic".

Anthropic said on Friday evening that it had yet to hear anything directly from the White House or the military "on the status of our negotiations".

Nevertheless, the company said being designated a supply chain risk "would both be legally unsound and set a dangerous precedent for any American company that negotiates with the government".

"No amount of intimidation or punishment from the Department of War will change our position on mass domestic surveillance or fully autonomous weapons", the company added.

The Department of War is a secondary name Trump has given to the defence department.

Anthropic's tools will be phased out of all government work over the next six months, Trump said.

As for Anthropic's other customers, the company said the only impact will be on companies that also contract with the military. Those companies may have to stop using Anthropic for work being done on behalf of the department.

Prior to Trump's pronouncement, Anthropic had said that, if the US Department of Defense chose to stop using the company's tools, the company would "work to enable a smooth transition to another provider".

Yet, Trump berated the company on his Truth Social platform, saying Anthropic "better get their act together, and be helpful during this phase out period, or I will use the Full Power of the Presidency to make them comply, with major civil and criminal consequences to follow".

Anthropic has been in use by the US government and military since 2024 and was the first advanced AI company to have its tools deployed in government agencies doing classified work.

Prior to Trump's decision on Friday, Anthropic received support in its stance against the government.

OpenAI boss Sam Altman offered support to rival AI executive Amodei. He sent a note to staff stating that he had the same "red lines" when it came to the application of the firms' products.

In the note seen by the BBC, Altman said any OpenAI contracts for the military would also reject uses that were "unlawful or unsuited to cloud deployments, such as domestic surveillance and autonomous offensive weapons".

He later confirmed on X that OpenAI had reached a deal with the Department of War to use its AI models on classified cloud networks.

Amodei is a long-time figure in tech, rising to prominence as an early employee of OpenAI. He and a handful of other OpenAI employees left the company to found Anthropic after disagreements with Altman.

The two startups now compete directly for users and corporate customers with an evolving offer of AI chatbots, agents and other tools.

"I do not fully understand how things got here; I do not know why Anthropic did their deal with the Pentagon and Palantir in the way they originally did it," Altman wrote in his company memo.

"But regardless of how we got here, this is no longer just an issue between Anthropic and the [Department of War] DoW; this is an issue for the whole industry and it is important to clarify our stance."

Hegseth on Tuesday called Amodei to Washington DC for a meeting amid growing tensions. It culminated in two contradictory ultimatums if Anthropic refused to grant the department free rein of its tools.

Hegseth said he would invoke the Defense Production Act, allowing the government to use Anthropic's products as it saw fit, and deem it a "supply chain risk".

By Thursday, Amodei said he would rather stop working with the Pentagon than acquiesce to such threats.

A former official with the DoD, who asked not to be named, told the BBC that Anthropic appeared to have the upper hand in the fight.

"This is great PR for them and they simply do not need the money," the former official said.

Anthropic's work with the Pentagon is part of a contract worth $200m (PS149m). The company's most recent valuation came earlier this month and put the company's worth at $380 billion, based on its current revenue and future expected earnings.

The former official added that the defence department's basis for threatening Anthropic with invoking either the Defense Production Act and being labelled a supply chain risk was "extremely flimsy".
